title: Eschatology: The Intermediate State After Death Before the Resurrection — WLC 86
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 3 on Eschatology_ The Intermediate State After Death Before the Resurrection - WLC 86.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Living Generation and the Tension of Christian Life
subpoints: One generation of Christians will not die — those alive at Christ's return will be transformed (1 Thessalonians 4:15-17)
There is no guarantee that the current generation is that final generation; every Christian generation has believed it would be last
We live with dual expectation: the return of the Lord and preparation to meet Him in death
WLC Q. 86 guides today's study: what is the communion in glory the invisible church enjoys immediately after death?

outline.title: Heaven Is for the Regenerate — The Invisible Church
subpoints: The catechism specifies 'members of the invisible church' — not the visible church broadly defined
The invisible church: all the elect throughout all ages, regenerated by the Spirit; boundaries known only to God
The visible church: professing Christians and their children at a given time — includes both believers and unbelievers
The parable of the wheat and tares confirms ongoing mixture in the visible church until the final judgment (Matthew 13)
Roman Catholicism and Federal Vision movement err by equating church membership with membership in heaven
Only those regenerated by the Holy Spirit and united to Christ by faith enjoy fellowship with God after death

outline.title: Souls Are Made Perfect in Holiness in Heaven
subpoints: Upon death, the souls of believers are freed from sin — the spiritual effects of the fall no longer apply
Perfection here does not mean omniscience — souls remain finite creatures; we will never exhaust the knowledge of the infinite God
We will be sinless — able to fellowship with God directly without the barrier of indwelling sin
Hebrews 12:22-23: 'the spirits of just men made perfect' — the gathering in heaven described
Revelation 14:13: those who die in the Lord rest from their labors
This is what Christ is doing in sanctifying his church — to present her blameless and perfect

outline.title: The Souls in Heaven Behold God and Wait for the Resurrection
subpoints: The catechism says souls 'behold the face of God in light and glory' — obviously figurative since souls have no eyes, but describing intimate fellowship
Philippians 1:23: to depart and be with Christ is 'far better' than the present life
Psalm 16:11: 'In your presence is fullness of joy; at your right hand are pleasures forevermore'
The intermediate state is not the ultimate goal — souls are waiting for the full redemption of their bodies
The New Testament use of 'salvation' includes the entire arc from regeneration through glorification at the resurrection
Revelation 6:9-11: the souls of the martyrs under the throne cry out 'How long?' — actively longing for the resurrection
Romans 8: even those with the firstfruits of the Spirit groan for the adoption, the redemption of the body

outline.title: The Body and the Wicked in the Intermediate State
subpoints: Even in death, bodies remain united to Christ — the body in the grave belongs to Him, not to another
1 Corinthians 15:35-44: Paul uses the seed-and-harvest analogy — the same body sown will be raised in glory, power, and incorruption
Our resurrection body will be a human, glorified body — as Christ's resurrection body is the model
Revelation 20:4: John sees the souls of the martyrs who 'lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years'
The wicked go to hell at death — the intermediate state for them is a conscious holding period before the final resurrection and judgment
Revelation 20:5: the rest of the dead do not live again until the thousand years are finished
John 5:28-29: two resurrections — of life and of condemnation — both involving the same Greek word for 'lived'

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro opens this third lesson by reminding the class that one generation of Christians will not die — those alive at the Lord's return will be transformed without dying (1 Thessalonians 4:15-17). With that, he frames the enduring tension of Christian life: we live looking for the Lord's return while simultaneously preparing to meet Him in death. The guide for today's study is Westminster Larger Catechism Q. 86, amended by the Bible Presbyterian Church to include reference to the millennial reign.

The lesson begins with a crucial clarification: heaven in the intermediate state is for the regenerate — specifically the invisible church, the collection of all elect souls throughout history. This is not identical to the visible church, which contains both believers and unbelievers (as Jesus taught in the parable of the wheat and tares). Groups that equate church membership with heavenly membership, such as Roman Catholicism and Federal Vision proponents, therefore err in a significant way. Only those truly regenerated by the Holy Spirit and united to Christ by faith enjoy communion with God after death.

Upon death, the souls of believers are made perfect in holiness — freed from indwelling sin and therefore able to fellowship with God directly. This perfection does not mean omniscience; souls remain finite creatures. But they are morally perfect, they are in intimate fellowship with Christ, and they are resting. Hebrews 12:22-23, Revelation 14:13, and Philippians 1:23 all point to this blessed state. However, the pastor insists that this is not the ultimate destination: the intermediate state is only desirable because it is temporary. The souls of the martyrs in Revelation 6 are actively longing and crying out for the resurrection, and Paul in 2 Corinthians 5 describes his bodiless state as 'nakedness' — something he does not desire permanently.

The lesson closes by affirming that even bodies in the grave remain united to Christ awaiting the resurrection, and that for the wicked, the intermediate state is a conscious holding period before the resurrection of condemnation described in John 5:28-29 and Revelation 20:5.
